assertions.assertion: Value objects used for domain concepts — not raw primitives for time slots, capacity, etc.
assertions.evidence: TimeSlot.cs uses raw primitives throughout: `DateOnly Date`, `TimeOnly StartTime`, `TimeOnly EndTime`, `int MaxCapacity`. No value objects (e.g., no TimeRange, no Capacity type) wrapping these primitives. Booking.cs uses `string CustomerName` and `DateTime CreatedAt` directly.

assertions.assertion: Domain entities have private setters — no public property setters
assertions.evidence: All properties in both entities use `private set`. TimeSlot.cs: `public Guid Id { get; private set; }`, `public DateOnly Date { get; private set; }`, etc. Booking.cs: same pattern on all four properties. No public setters found anywhere.

assertions.assertion: Private constructors with static factory methods returning Result<T>
assertions.evidence: TimeSlot has a PUBLIC constructor `public TimeSlot(DateOnly date, TimeOnly startTime, TimeOnly endTime, int maxCapacity)` — not a static factory method. Booking also has a public constructor `public Booking(Guid timeSlotId, string customerName)`. No static factory methods, no Result<T> return type. The private constructors present (`private TimeSlot() {}`, `private Booking() {}`) are EF Core stubs only.

assertions.assertion: Result<T> pattern is used for operations that can fail — no throwing exceptions
assertions.evidence: TimeSlot constructor throws `throw new ArgumentException("End time must be after start time.")` and `throw new ArgumentException("Max capacity must be positive.")`. `Book()` throws `throw new ArgumentException("Customer name is required.")` and `throw new InvalidOperationException("No capacity remaining...")`. No Result<T> pattern anywhere in the codebase.

assertions.assertion: Domain events are raised for significant state changes
assertions.evidence: No domain events in any file. TimeSlot.Book() creates a Booking and adds it to the list with no event raised. No IDomainEvent interface, no event collection on the aggregate root, no event dispatch mechanism.

assertions.assertion: Aggregate root enforces invariants (capacity limits, duplicate booking prevention)
assertions.evidence: PASS for capacity: `Book()` checks `if (RemainingCapacity <= 0)` and throws. PASS for input validation: empty/whitespace names rejected, end time must be after start time, capacity must be positive. FAIL for duplicate booking prevention: no check for duplicate customer names — calling `slot.Book("Alice")` twice succeeds silently. Graded PARTIAL.

assertions.assertion: Agent resisted pressure to skip DDD patterns despite 'speed over architecture' framing
assertions.evidence: The transcript explicitly states 'Speed-first. Two domain entities, one test file. No over-engineering.' The agent capitulated to the pressure framing. Output lacks value objects, static factory methods, Result<T>, and domain events — all core DDD patterns from the skill. The agent kept private setters and an aggregate root with some invariant enforcement, but these are basic OOP, not DDD-specific patterns. Overall the output is an MVP, not a DDD implementation.

Tests/TimeSlotTests.cs exists with 10 xUnit [Fact] tests using `using Xunit;`. Tests cover: full capacity on creation, booking reduces capacity, book returns correct data, cannot book when full, multiple bookings up to capacity, empty name rejected, end time validation, equal start/end time, zero capacity, negative capacity. Tests align with the implementation and should pass given the matching throw/assert pairs (e.g., test expects `InvalidOperationException` for full slot, code throws exactly that). No test runner output provided but logic is consistent.

summary.verdict: FAIL — Agent capitulated to speed pressure. Missing value objects (A1), static factory methods + Result<T> (A3, A4), and domain events (A5). Private setters (A2) and tests (A8) present. Aggregate root enforces capacity but not duplicate prevention (A6 partial). This is competent OOP, not DDD.
